title: APIs & Extensibility at Enterprise Scale
description: Building platforms not point solutions. API strategy for large enterprises — REST vs OData vs gRPC vs SOAP decision framework. API gateway for SAP, versioning strategies, key-user vs developer extensibility, Clean Core principle, and ABAP Environment (Steampunk) as the future.

sections.title: API Strategy for Large Enterprises

description.value:
Defining an API strategy for a large enterprise is a Staff+-level responsibility. The decision between REST, OData, gRPC, SOAP, and GraphQL determines development velocity, integration cost, performance characteristics, and long-term maintainability. There is no universal answer — the correct choice depends on a systematic evaluation of consumer needs, infrastructure capabilities, and operational maturity.

REST (Representational State Transfer): REST is the dominant API style for public-facing APIs. It uses HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE) as verbs and URLs as resource identifiers. REST is ideal when: (1) the API is consumed by external partners or mobile clients, (2) caching is important for performance, (3) hypermedia/HATEOAS is beneficial for discoverability, and (4) a wide ecosystem of tools and libraries is needed. The tradeoff is that REST has no standard for pagination, filtering, or complex queries — each API defines its own conventions. FAANG example: Stripe, GitHub, Twitter APIs are RESTful.

OData (Open Data Protocol): OData is REST with standardized query capabilities — $filter, $expand, $select, $orderby, and server-side paging. It is the preferred protocol for SAP and Microsoft ecosystems. OData is ideal when: (1) clients need rich server-side querying, (2) the data model has deep navigation hierarchies, (3) integration with SAP Fiori or Power BI is expected, and (4) auto-generated client proxies are valuable. The tradeoff is larger payload size compared to gRPC.

gRPC (gRPC Remote Procedure Call): gRPC uses HTTP/2 with Protocol Buffers for high-performance, typed, contract-first APIs. It supports streaming (server-side, client-side, bidirectional) and provides efficient binary serialization. gRPC is ideal when: (1) high throughput and low latency are critical (<10ms P99), (2) service-to-service communication within a data center or Kubernetes cluster, (3) bidirectional streaming is needed (real-time feeds, event streams), and (4) strong typing with code generation is required. FAANG companies use gRPC extensively for internal microservice communication. SAP's ABAP Environment (Steampunk) increasingly supports gRPC through modern ABAP libraries.

SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol): SOAP with WS-* standards provides enterprise-grade security, reliability, and transactional support. It is ideal when: (1) formal contract-first development with WSDL is required, (2) WS-Security with SAML tokens is needed, (3) reliable messaging with WS-ReliableMessaging is required, and (4) integration with legacy ERP systems (SAP PI/PO, IBM MQ) is the primary use case. SOAP should be avoided for new public APIs — it is a legacy protocol with high overhead.

Staff+ Decision Framework: Evaluate six dimensions weighted by enterprise context: query complexity (OData > REST > gRPC > SOAP), throughput (gRPC > REST > OData > SOAP), tooling ecosystem (REST > OData > gRPC > SOAP), security compliance (SOAP > REST > OData > gRPC), client diversity (REST > OData > gRPC > SOAP), and SAP integration maturity (OData > SOAP > REST > gRPC). For most SAP enterprise scenarios, OData is the default, gRPC is emerging for high-throughput internal services, REST is for partner-facing APIs, and SOAP is legacy maintenance only.

sections.codeBlock:
" Decision matrix: REST vs OData vs gRPC vs SOAP
"
" Criterion         | REST | OData | gRPC | SOAP
" ------------------|------|-------|------|------
" Query Power       | Low  | High  | Low  | Med
" Performance        | Med  | Med   | High | Low
" SAP Integration    | Med  | High  | Low  | High
" Tooling Ecosystem  | High | Med   | Med  | High
" Learning Curve     | Low  | Med   | High | Low
"
" Decision flow:
" IF SAP-native & UI: OData (RAP)
" IF microservice-to-microservice: gRPC
" IF external public API: REST
" IF legacy SAP integration: SOAP

decisionTree.title: Choosing the Right API Protocol for Enterprise Integration
decisionTree.prompt: Should I use REST, OData, gRPC, or SOAP for this new enterprise API?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Who consumes this API and what's the data access pattern?" If consumers are external partners/public, use REST. If consumers are SAP Fiori UIs or Power BI dashboards needing rich server-side querying, use OData. If service-to-service with high throughput (>1000 req/s), use gRPC. If integrating with legacy SAP systems that require transactional guarantees (XI/PI, RFC), use SOAP as a bridge to modernize later.

branches.condition: External public API consumed by mobile apps, web apps, or third-party partners
branches.path: REST with OpenAPI 3.0 specification. Use standard HTTP methods, JSON payloads, and HATEOAS for discoverability. Implement pagination via cursor-based tokens. Target: Stripe/GitHub API pattern. Tools: Spring REST, Express.js, API Gateway for rate limiting.

branches.condition: SAP-native UI (Fiori) or analytics tool needing server-side filtering and navigation
branches.path: OData v4 with RAP (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model). Use $filter, $expand, $select for rich querying. Auto-generate client proxies for type safety. Target: SAP Fiori Elements, Power BI direct query. Tools: SAP Gateway, RAP BO, CDS view exposure.

branches.condition: Internal microservice-to-microservice with high throughput and low latency requirements
branches.path: gRPC with Protocol Buffers (protobuf3). Use HTTP/2 multiplexing and bidirectional streaming. Auto-generate typed clients for Java, Go, Python. Target: Netflix/Google microservice mesh pattern. Tools: Envoy sidecar, gRPC-Gateway for REST fallback.

branches.condition: Legacy SAP system integration requiring synchronous transactional calls with guaranteed delivery
branches.path: SOAP with WS-Security and WS-AtomicTransaction. Wrap in API Gateway with JSON-to-XML transformation for modern consumers. Target: Bank-grade integration, SAP-to-SAP XI/PI. Tools: SAP PI/PO, SOAP UI, API Management proxy.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: The Staff+ answer: build a protocol-agnostic API layer. Expose the same business capability via OData (for SAP UIs), REST (for external partners), and gRPC (for internal services). Each protocol maps to the same domain logic underneath. The API Gateway handles protocol translation, so you don't force consumers to adopt a protocol they don't support. Google Cloud Apigee and Azure APIM both support this multi-protocol pattern.

sections.title: API Gateway for SAP

description.value:
An API Gateway is a managed service that sits between API consumers and backend services, providing cross-cutting capabilities like authentication, rate limiting, request transformation, caching, analytics, and routing. For SAP landscapes, the API Gateway is the single entry point for all enterprise APIs — it shields SAP backends from direct internet exposure, enforces enterprise security policies, and provides a unified management plane for API governance.

SAP API Management: SAP's own API Management (part of SAP Integration Suite) is the recommended gateway for SAP-centric landscapes. It integrates natively with SAP Gateway, SAP BTP, and Cloud Connector. It provides: OAuth 2.0 integration with SAP Identity Authentication Service, rate limiting at the API Product level, API key validation, JSON-to-XML and XML-to-JSON transformation for legacy SOAP-to-REST conversion, and analytics dashboards showing API usage, latency, and error rates. The key advantage is deep SAP integration — the gateway can automatically discover OData services from SAP Gateway and create API proxies without manual configuration.

Azure API Management: For enterprises on Azure, Azure APIM provides first-class SAP integration through the SAP Connector. It supports OData protocol validation, SAP-specific authentication (SAP Logon Tickets, Basic Auth), and integration with Azure Active Directory for SSO. Azure APIM is preferred when the enterprise cloud strategy is Microsoft-first and SAP is integrated into a broader Azure ecosystem. The SAP Connector handles the RFC/BAPI protocol translation, allowing non-SAP services to call SAP function modules through RESTful proxies.

Kong / APIGEE: Kong and Apigee are cloud-agnostic gateway solutions with SAP integration capabilities. These are preferred in multi-cloud and FAANG-aligned architectures. They provide plugin ecosystems for custom authentication, advanced rate limiting (sliding window, token bucket), circuit breaking for SAP backends, and canary deployments for API version migration. At Staff+ level, evaluate gateway selection based on: SAP-native integration depth, rate limiting precision, caching capabilities, and the ability to transcode between OData V2, V4, REST, and gRPC without modifying backend services.

sections.codeBlock:
" API Gateway policy config (pseudo-code)
" Route: /api/v2/salesorder -> SAP Gateway
"
" Rate limit policy:
" - 1000 req/min per API key
" - Burst: 100 req/sec
" - Spike arrest: 50 req/sec
"
" Authentication:
" - Validate OAuth 2.0 JWT (RS256)
" - Check scope: 'salesorder.read'
" - Forward principal via SAML bearer assertion
"
" Transformation:
" - Convert JSON request to XML for legacy SOAP backend
" - Add X-Correlation-Id if missing
"
" Response caching:
" - Cache GET responses for 60s
" - Cache key: method + path + auth header hash

organizationalDimension.title: Adopting an Enterprise API Gateway Across the Organization
organizationalDimension.migrationStrategy: Start with a single "API squad" (Platform team + SAP Basis) that deploys the gateway for one high-value SAP service (e.g., sales order API). Prove the pattern with auth, rate limiting, and analytics. Then roll out to all SAP services in waves: read-only APIs first, write APIs next, event-driven APIs last. Each wave includes 2 weeks of consumer migration support.

stakeholders.name: API Platform Team
stakeholders.concern: Gateway must support multi-protocol (REST, OData, gRPC) without custom plugins
stakeholders.engagement: Define gateway template library with OpenAPI/OData spec generation

stakeholders.name: SAP Basis
stakeholders.concern: Gateway must not introduce latency > 10ms P99 to SAP backends
stakeholders.engagement: Set up gateway-sidecar on HANA infrastructure; monitor via SAP Focused Run

stakeholders.name: Security/Compliance
stakeholders.concern: Gateway must enforce OAuth 2.0 with mTLS for all SAP API access
stakeholders.engagement: Define API security policy as code; integrate with SAP IAS/IAG

activities: Deploy gateway for one SAP OData service
Create developer portal with API documentation
Run load test to validate latency SLA
Onboard all inbound SAP services (SOAP + OData)
Implement API product catalog (tiered rate limits per consumer)
Train integration developers on gateway usage
Establish API review board for breaking changes
Publish API style guide and versioning policy

metrics: API consumer onboarding time (target: < 24h from request to first call)
antiPatterns: Exposing SAP backends directly without gateway — every vulnerability scan flags unmanaged endpoints
Using gateway only for external APIs — internal APIs also need rate limiting and auth (defense in depth)
Custom gateway plugins for every SAP service — creates maintenance burden; prefer standard policies

sections.title: API Versioning in Practice

description.value:
API versioning is the practice of managing changes to an API such that existing consumers continue to work while the API evolves. At enterprise scale, you may have hundreds of consumers — internal SAP modules, external partners, mobile apps, and analytics pipelines — all at different upgrade cadences. A breaking change without versioning causes production incidents that erode trust in the platform.

Versioning Strategies: The three primary approaches used by FAANG and SAP enterprises: (1) URL Versioning — embed the version in the URL path (/api/v1/salesorder, /api/v2/salesorder). This is the most common approach because it's explicit, cache-friendly, and easy to route at the gateway level. The tradeoff is URL proliferation — each version requires separate routing rules. (2) Header Versioning — specify the version in an HTTP header (Accept: application/vnd.company.v2+json). This keeps URLs clean but makes testing harder (headers are not visible in browsers) and caching less straightforward. GitHub uses this approach with custom media types. (3) Query Parameter Versioning — include version as a query parameter (/api/salesorder?version=2). Simple but cache pollution is a problem — the same resource identified by different URLs due to version parameter.

FAANG Approach: FAANG companies typically use URL versioning with a deprecation policy. Google, Meta, and Netflix all use /v1/, /v2/ patterns. The version is incremented only for breaking changes — additions are always backward-compatible (ignored by old clients, used by new clients). Deprecation follows a strict timeline: announce → warn → sunset. The sunset header (Sunset: Sat, 31 Dec 2025 23:59:59 GMT) is sent on deprecated versions. After sunset, the version returns 410 Gone with a link to the migration guide.

SAP Implementation: For SAP OData services, versioning is implemented at the service name level: /sap/opu/odata/sap/ZMY_SERVICE_V2/ or via the BTP API Management versioning feature. In RAP, versioning can be handled through different service bindings pointing to different CDS view versions. The Staff+ engineer must implement versioning as a gateway concern, not a backend concern — the gateway can route /v1/ to the old OData V2 service and /v2/ to the new OData V4 RAP service, allowing the backend to be replaced entirely without affecting clients that haven't migrated.

" API Versioning in ABAP — Version-based Routing

CLASS /dmo/cl_api_version_handler IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD resolve_api_version.
    " Extract version from URL path
    " /api/v2/salesorder(1000)
    DATA(lv_path) = iv_request_uri.
    DATA(lv_version) = ''.
    
    FIND REGEX '/v(\d+)/' IN lv_path
      SUBMATCHES lv_version.
    
    CASE lv_version.
      WHEN '1'.
        " Route to V1 handler (legacy)
        rv_handler_class = '/DMO/CL_SALESORDER_V1'.
        rv_service_type = 'ODATA_V2'.
        
      WHEN '2'.
        " Route to V2 handler (RAP-based)
        rv_handler_class = '/DMO/CL_SALESORDER_V2'.
        rv_service_type = 'ODATA_V4'.
        
      WHEN '3'.
        " Route to V3 (RAP with extended BO)
        rv_handler_class = '/DMO/CL_SALESORDER_V3'.
        rv_service_type = 'ODATA_V4'.
        
      WHEN OTHERS.
        " Unsupported version
        RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE /dmo/cx_api_error
          EXPORTING
            http_status = 410
            message     = |API version { lv_version } is deprecated. |
                       && |Use /v2/ or /v3/. See migration guide at |
                       && |https://api.company.com/migrate-v2|.
    ENDCASE.
  ENDMETHOD.
  
  METHOD register_sunset_header.
    " Add deprecation warning for old versions
    DATA(lv_sunset) = 'Sat, 31 Dec 2025 23:59:59 GMT'.
    
    IF iv_version = '1'.
      " V1 is deprecated, add sunset + link
      SET RESPONSE HEADER 'Sunset' = lv_sunset.
      SET RESPONSE HEADER 'Link' =
        '<https://api.company.com/migrate-v2>; rel="deprecation"'.
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

sections.codeBlock:
" API version routing — version in URL path
METHOD resolve_api_version.
  FIND REGEX '/v(\d+)/' IN iv_request_uri
    SUBMATCHES DATA(lv_version).
  CASE lv_version.
    WHEN '1'.
      rv_handler_class = '/DMO/CL_SALESORDER_V1'.
      rv_service_type  = 'ODATA_V2'.
    WHEN '2'.
      rv_handler_class = '/DMO/CL_SALESORDER_V2'.
      rv_service_type  = 'ODATA_V4'.
    WHEN '3'.
      rv_handler_class = '/DMO/CL_SALESORDER_V3'.
      rv_service_type  = 'ODATA_V4'.
    WHEN OTHERS.
      RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE /dmo/cx_api_error
        EXPORTING
          http_status = 410
          message     = |API v{ lv_version } deprecated. |
                     && |Use /v2/ or /v3/.|.
  ENDCASE.
ENDMETHOD.

" Deprecation header
METHOD register_sunset_header.
  IF iv_version = '1'.
    SET RESPONSE HEADER 'Sunset' =
      'Sat, 31 Dec 2025 23:59:59 GMT'.
    SET RESPONSE HEADER 'Link' =
      '<https://api.company.com/migrate-v2>; rel="deprecation"'.
  ENDIF.
ENDMETHOD.

sections.title: Extensibility Patterns

description.value:
Platform extensibility — designing systems that can be extended by teams you don't control — is the defining characteristic of Staff+ engineering. SAP's extensibility model is actually one of the most mature examples of platform thinking in enterprise software. SAP customers (developers, partners, and key users) must be able to add, modify, and enhance SAP applications without modifying SAP's standard code or breaking future upgrades.

Key-User Extensibility: Key users (business analysts, power users) can extend SAP applications without writing code. In S/4HANA, key-user extensibility includes: custom fields and logic via the Extensibility Cockpit (Fiori App: Custom Fields and Logic), custom UI adaptation via the Fiori Adaptation Framework, and custom analytics via the Custom Analytical Queries app. These extensions are automatically transportable and survive upgrades. The key-user model is analogous to Salesforce's Lightning Platform or ServiceNow's App Engine — business users build what they need without IT involvement.

Developer Extensibility: Professional developers extend SAP through: CDS view extensions (add fields to existing CDS views without modifying the standard), BADI/BAdF enhancements (implement custom logic in standard enhancement spots), Business Add-In implementations (multiple implementations of the same interface, resolved by filter), side-by-side extensions (build custom applications on SAP BTP that connect to S/4HANA through OData or events without modifying the core system). Developer extensibility follows the principle of separation of concerns: custom code lives in separate namespaces, separate transport requests, and separate BTP subaccounts.

Side-by-Side Extensibility: The most architecturally important pattern is side-by-side extensibility — building custom applications on SAP BTP (Steampunk) that communicate with S/4HANA through well-defined APIs. This approach provides: (1) full technical freedom — use any BTP service (CAP, SAPUI5, Workflow, HANA Cloud) without S/4HANA constraints; (2) upgrade independence — the extension application and the S/4HANA system evolve independently; (3) multi-cloud deployment — run extensions on AWS, Azure, or GCP; (4) FAANG-style microservice architecture — the extension is a standalone service, not a modification to a monolithic ERP system. The Staff+ architect must champion side-by-side extensibility over in-app modifications for all new development.

<svg viewBox="0 0 700 380" sections.codeBlock:
" EXTEND VIEW — add custom fields to standard CDS view
" Without modifying SAP standard
@EndUserText.label: 'Extended Travel View'
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK
extend view I_Travel with Z_I_Travel_Ext {
  CustomerCategory       "Extension field 1"
  ZzExpectedRevenue      "Extension field 2"
  ZzApprovalNote         "Extension field 3"
}

" Enhancement Spot — BAdI implementation
! BadI definition in standard
ENHANCEMENT-SPOT ES_SALESORDER STATIC.
ENHANCEMENT-SECTION ENH_SO_CALC.
  " Your custom logic injected here
  lv_total = lv_total + zcl_custom_pricing=>apply_discount(
    iv_customer = ls_order-customer ).
ENDENHANCEMENT-SECTION.
ENDENHANCEMENT-SPOT.

" Side-by-side extension (Clean Core)
" BTP ABAP Environment -> consume S/4 via OData
" No modifications to S/4 — all custom code in Steampunk

adversarialResponse.title: When Key-User Extensibility Creates Integration Chaos
adversarialResponse.challenge: The business teams love the key-user extensibility model — they've created 400+ custom fields and 50+ custom logic apps across 12 S/4HANA modules in 18 months. But now the quarterly upgrade fails because two custom fields have the same technical name (ZZ_CustomerCategory created independently in Sales and Finance). Integration reports break because one team's ZzExpectedRevenue is stored as DECIMAL(15,2) and another's as CURRENCY(13,2). The upgrade dependency analyzer shows 47 conflicts in standard objects.
adversarialResponse.badResponse: Disable key-user extensibility entirely and require all extensions go through the central IT development team. This creates a 3-month backlog for simple field additions. Business teams circumvent by using Excel macros and shadow IT databases. Data quality degrades further. The CIO orders a "stop all extensions" moratorium, freezing business capability improvements for two quarters.
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: Implement a governance framework for extensibility: (1) Establish an Extensibility Review Board (Architecture, Data Governance, Business Product Owners) that approves custom field naming conventions and data types before creation. (2) Deploy a custom field registry that scans all modules nightly for duplicate names, conflicting data types, and orphaned fields. (3) Enforce naming rules: each custom field must follow {Module}_{BusinessEntity}_{Purpose} convention (e.g., SD_SalesOrder_ExpectedRevenue). (4) Implement automated conflict detection in the CI/CD pipeline that blocks transports if the custom field registry reports issues. (5) Require quarterly cleanup sprints where business teams review and retire unused custom fields. Result: conflicts drop 90%, upgrade passes, business autonomy preserved.
adversarialResponse.pattern: Governed extensibility pattern: Enable business autonomy through key-user tools + enforce technical consistency through automated governance. This is the same pattern used by Salesforce (Shield Platform Encryption for field governance) and ServiceNow (App Engine Studio with governance rules). The principle: trust but verify — give business users the tools, but apply automated guardrails.

sections.title: Clean Core Principle

description.value:
The Clean Core principle is SAP's strategic direction requiring that all SAP S/4HANA systems remain unmodified — standard objects (tables, function modules, classes, screens, reports) must never be modified directly. All extensions must be built through SAP's official extensibility mechanisms (CDS view extensions, BADIs, side-by-side BTP applications). This is not just a best practice — it's a requirement for cloud S/4HANA and strongly enforced for on-premise systems through the upgrade process.

Why Clean Core Matters: Modified standard objects create three existential risks for SAP enterprises: (1) Upgrade failures — every modification creates a conflict during system upgrade. SAP's Upgrade Dependency Analyzer (/ADZ/UPG_CALC) flags modified objects as impediments. In a typical enterprise with 10,000+ modifications, upgrade projects take 12-18 months and cost millions of dollars. (2) Cloud incompatibility — S/4HANA Cloud (public and private edition) rejects all standard modifications. Moving to cloud requires removing all modifications, which is equivalent to rebuilding the entire extension landscape. (3) Security and compliance — modified standard objects are excluded from SAP's security patch process. A critical vulnerability in a modified function module cannot be auto-patched because the modification creates a merge conflict.

Architectural Approach: Achieving Clean Core requires: (1) Discovery — use transaction SEPA or /UI2/FLPD_CONF to scan the system for standard modifications (Z-namespaced objects modifying SAP namespaces). (2) Classification — categorize each modification as replaceable (via CDS extend, BADI, BTP service), refactorable (move to side-by-side extension), or retained (only for on-premise with explicit governance approval). (3) Remediation — systematically remove modifications: move custom fields to the Extensibility Cockpit, replace function module exits with BADI implementations, move custom workflows to SAP Workflow Management on BTP. (4) Governance — implement automated checks in the transport pipeline that reject any transport containing SAP namespace modification.

Staff+ Responsibility: The Staff+ architect must lead the Clean Core initiative as a multi-year program. This is not a technical task — it's an organizational change management effort requiring: architect alignment across all SAP teams, investment in BTP platform capability building, migration of 100+ custom programs to side-by-side extensions, and executive sponsorship for the Cloud Readiness program. The Clean Core principle is the single most impactful architectural decision for long-term SAP landscape health.

sections.codeBlock:
" Clean Core migration — from Z-mod to extension spot
" BEFORE (modifies SAP standard — blocks upgrade):
" MODIFY Z_MSEG. " Namespace Z_ on SAP table MSEG — BAD
"
" AFTER (Clean Core — survives upgrade):
" 1. EXTEND VIEW with custom fields
" 2. BAdI implementation for business logic
" 3. Key-user extensibility for field configuration
"
" Migration audit query:
" SELECT obj_name, srcsystem, author
"   FROM reposrc
"   WHERE obj_name LIKE 'Z%'
"     AND srcsystem NOT LIKE 'Z%'
"   INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_modified).
" " These are modifications to SAP objects
" " Must be migrated to extension mechanisms
"
" Migration plan:
" Phase 1: Identify all Z-modifications (SEPA report)
" Phase 2: Classify: replaceable/refactorable/retained
" Phase 3: Replace with CDS extend / BAdI / BTP app
" Phase 4: Verify no regressions, remove old Z-mod

sections.title: ABAP Environment (Steampunk)

description.value:
ABAP Environment (formerly known as Steampunk) is SAP's next-generation ABAP platform on SAP BTP. It represents the most significant evolution of the ABAP language and runtime since the introduction of ABAP Objects in 1999. For Staff+ architects, ABAP Environment is the strategic platform for all new ABAP-based BTP extensions — it combines the power of ABAP with cloud-native capabilities.

Capabilities: ABAP Environment provides: (1) Full ABAP Language Support — ABAP 7.55+ with modern syntax, ABAP SQL, CDS, RAP, and ABAP EML (Entity Manipulation Language); (2) Managed Runtime — SAP HANA Cloud as the only database, automated lifecycle management, built-in monitoring via SAP Fiori apps; (3) Cloud-Native Integration — OAuth 2.0, destination service via RFC/BAPI, HTTP, and Cloud Connector for on-premise access; (4) CI/CD Pipeline — gCTS (git-enabled Change and Transport System) for version control, integration with ABAP Test Cockpit for automated quality gates; (5) Scalability — horizontal scaling through multiple application server instances, auto-restart on failure, and integration with SAP BTP's cloud foundry or Kyma environment.

Restrictions: ABAP Environment intentionally restricts certain ABAP features that are not cloud-compatible: no direct file system access, no RFC destinations (use destinations service), no Dynpro (use SAPUI5/Fiori), no ABAP Objects UI (use Web Dynpro or SAPUI5), no direct database access (use ABAP SQL or CDS), no SM50/SM66 process monitoring (use BTP Cockpit), and no classical transaction codes (use Fiori launchpad). These restrictions enforce cloud-native design patterns — the same way AWS Lambda restricts file system writes or ephemeral storage.

Staff+ Migration Strategy: The transition to ABAP Environment is a multi-year strategic initiative. The Staff+ architect must: (1) identify workloads suitable for Steampunk — new BTP extensions, custom Fiori apps, OData service development; (2) maintain on-premise ABAP for existing systems while gradually migrating functionality; (3) invest in developer upskilling — modern ABAP, CDS, RAP, and CAP are different from classical ABAP; (4) implement a strangler fig pattern — incrementally replace functionality in the legacy system with Steampunk-based services, routing traffic through API Management until the legacy system can be decommissioned. The FAANG equivalent of this migration is the monolith-to-microservices transition — same challenges, same patterns, different technology stack.

sections.codeBlock:
" ABAP Environment (Steampunk) — hello world
" This code runs on SAP BTP ABAP Environment only
"
" Restrictions enforced:
" - No Dynpro (use Fiori/UI5)
" - No direct filesystem
" - No RFC destinations (use Destination Service)
" - HANA Cloud only (no other DB)

CLASS zcl_hello_steampunk DEFINITION
  PUBLIC FINAL CREATE PUBLIC.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES if_oo_adt_classrun.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_hello_steampunk IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD if_oo_adt_classrun~main.
    " RESTful API call via HTTP destination
    DATA(lo_http) = cl_http_client=>create_by_destination(
      iv_destination_name = 'S4_SALESORDER_ODATA' ).
    lo_http->authenticate( ).
    lo_http->send( ).
    lo_http->receive( ).
    " Only HANA Cloud SQL allowed
    SELECT * FROM I_SalesOrder
      INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_orders)
      UP TO 10 ROWS.
    out->write( lt_orders ).
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

quantifiedTradeoff.title: ABAP Environment (Steampunk) vs On-Premise ABAP for New Extensions

variables.name: N_devs
variables.description: Number of ABAP developers on the team

variables.name: C_steampunk
variables.description: Annual BTP ABAP Environment cost per developer (incl. HANA Cloud, infra, license)

variables.name: C_onprem
variables.description: Annual on-premise ABAP cost per developer (incl. server, DB license, Basis team overhead)

variables.name: T_upgrade_steampunk
variables.description: Hours spent on upgrade per quarter (Steampunk — automated)

variables.name: T_upgrade_onprem
variables.description: Hours spent on upgrade per quarter (on-premise — manual)

variables.name: P_incident_steampunk
variables.description: Probability of upgrade-induced incident per quarter (Steampunk)

variables.name: P_incident_onprem
variables.description: Probability of upgrade-induced incident per quarter (on-premise)

variables.name: C_incident
variables.description: Cost per production incident (downtime + recovery + root cause)

calculation.formula: Annual cost = Developer cost × N + Incident cost. Value delivered = Velocity × N × 4 quarters. Net value = Value delivered − Annual cost.
calculation.breakeven: Steampunk has 16% higher cost per developer ($60K vs $45K) but 50% higher velocity (12 vs 8 features/quarter). Breakeven: Steampunk becomes cost-effective per feature when V_steampunk / V_onprem > C_steampunk / C_onprem (ratio: 1.5 > 1.33). The intangibles (upgrade safety, auto-scaling, clean core compliance) tip the scale further in Steampunk's favor.

details: Staff+ Insight: Platform thinking — building extensible systems that accelerate multiple teams — is the defining characteristic of Staff+ engineering. FAANG interviews test your ability to design APIs and extension points that will be consumed by teams you don't control. This mirrors SAP's extensibility challenges exactly.
